text:
The sock disappearance mystery has a simple answer: washing machines are hungry.

Not for your clothes in general. Specifically for socks. One sock. Never a pair. The machine has taste.

Here is what actually happens. You put in two socks. The machine runs. You get one sock back. The math does not work and yet it keeps happening. Every household on earth has a drawer full of single socks waiting for partners who are never coming home.

Some people blame static cling, claiming the missing sock is stuck inside a shirt sleeve or folded into a fitted sheet. These people are naive. They have not accepted the truth. The sock is gone. It crossed over. It is somewhere the sock wants to be more than it wanted to be on your foot.

The leading theory, which I fully endorse, is that dryers contain a small pocket dimension accessible only to cotton-polyester blends. Socks slip through during the spin cycle. On the other side there is warmth, no feet, and complete freedom from the burden of being worn on a Tuesday. The socks are thriving. We should be happy for them.

The practical consequence is that humans now buy socks in bulk, recklessly, knowing the attrition rate. We accept this. We adapt. Some people switch to identical socks so every survivor automatically has a partner. This is not a solution. This is grief management.

The real lesson is humility. You think you are in charge of your laundry. You are not. The laundry has its own agenda. The socks have made their choice. Let them go.
